coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of red emissive carbon quantum dots for MB degradation

Khojieva, Gulmira
Chen, Dan
Chen, Jun

Liu, Jing
Li, Yu
coredata.dc:description: In this study, red-emissive carbon quantum dots (R-CQDs) were synthesized using a simple, one-step solvothermal method with 1,3-naphthalenediol as the carbon source. The synthesized R-CQDs were thoroughly characterized in terms of morphology, crystallinity, functional groups, optical, and photocatalytic properties. The R-CQDs exhibited an average particle size of 5 nm, with functional groups aiding in their uniform dispersion in water. Raman spectra revealed the presence of both D and G bands, indicating successful construction of a sp2 core and sp3 hybridized matrix. The R-CQDs showed strong absorption at 512 nm and high emission intensity at 610 nm. Additionally, the R-CQDs demonstrated exceptional photocatalytic performance, achieving 96 % methylene blue degradation in 180 min, with a calculated rate constant of 0.0167 min−1. Their stability across different pH levels further underscores their potential as efficient photocatalysts in a variety of environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emissive carbon quantum dots
Up-conversion
photoluminescence, MB degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic demonstration of the synthesis process for R-CQDs. (b) The typical HRTEM image, as well as (c) XRD patterns of R-CQDs. The insets in (b) are the size distribution of R-CQDs and a photograph of R-CQDs in ethanol, respectively.
(a) FTIR spectrum; (b) Raman spectrum of R-CQDs; (c) XPS survey spectrum; (d) C 1 s and (f) XPS spectra of O1s, respectively.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of R-CQDs. The PL spectra of R-CQDs are shown in the inset, demonstrating their excitation-dependent nature; (b) PL emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths; (c) Estimating the band gap of R-CQDs using the Kubelka-Munk function; (d) Mott–Schottky plot of R-CQDs.

(a) UV–vis spectrum of MB dye degraded by using R-CQDs; (b) the photocatalytic degradation rate of MB by R-CQDs under simulated visible light (with cut-of-filter λ > 420 nm); (c) degradation efficiency of MB; (d) kinetic study of MB for R-CQDs-visible light; (e) a plot of degradation efficiency versus time for MB under three different pH conditions by R-CQDs; (f) he proposed degradation process of MB catalysed by CQDs.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of red emissive carbon quantum dots for MB degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, red-emissive carbon quantum dots (R-CQDs) were synthesized using a simple, one-step solvothermal method with 1,3-naphthalenediol as the carbon source. The synthesized R-CQDs were thoroughly characterized in terms of morphology, crystallinity, functional groups, optical, and photocatalytic properties. The R-CQDs exhibited an average particle size of 5 nm, with functional groups aiding in their uniform dispersion in water. Raman spectra revealed the presence of both D and G bands, indicating successful construction of a sp2 core and sp3 hybridized matrix. The R-CQDs showed strong absorption at 512 nm and high emission intensity at 610 nm. Additionally, the R-CQDs demonstrated exceptional photocatalytic performance, achieving 96 % methylene blue degradation in 180 min, with a calculated rate constant of 0.0167 min−1. Their stability across different pH levels further underscores their potential as efficient photocatalysts in a variety of environmental applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Various fields such as photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, biosensing, and bioimaging extensively utilize CQDs due to their inexpensive nature, easy modification, adjustable fluorescence emission, strong resistance to light-induced degradation, and appropriate compatibility with living organisms [1]. The significant value of CQDs has led to the development of various techniques to create them [2]. Despite the discovery of numerous methods to produce CQDs due to their high value, these materials still require good optical properties due to the challenging conditions of their formation. CQDs display a variety of photoluminescence, exhibit properties that vary depending on the excitation, and lack very pure colors. These issues have made it challenging to use CQDs with very pure colors [3]. Thorough purification and separation of CQDs is crucial for the development of novel applications. The study investigates the development and potential applications of quantum dots based on unique properties such as UV light absorption, wavelength tunableness, quantum confinement [4,5].

CQDs, known for their unique properties, excellent photobleaching stability, minimal toxicity, and easy synthetic methods, have gained significant interest in recent years [6]. Fluorescence QY, a fundamental property of CQDs, is defined as the ratio of released photons to absorbed photons by the fluorophore [7]. The brightness of CQDs increases with an increase in quantum yield, and surface modification significantly influences absorption and photoluminescence properties. The synthesis methods for CQDs include chemical oxidation of carbon sources, thermal cracking, hydrothermal synthesis supported by microwaves, fast plasma pyrolysis, and electrolysis of graphite assisted using alkali [8]. The hydrothermal/solvothermal method is an excellent approach for achieving precise control over the form and size of CQDs, resulting in a high degree of fluorescence. Modifications or doping, the use of strong electron-donating or electron-accepting compounds, and surface modification or passivation through covalent bonding with amine-containing chemicals can all enhance hydrothermally prepared CQDs [9]. Doping, particularly N-doping CQDs, can significantly improve the PL emission by causing shift of band structure [10]. The mentioned works are currently limited due to the need for complex procedures or expensive passivating agents.

Tetsuka et al. have revealed that the molecular orbital resonance, which is produced by the delocalized p orbital of GQDs, may be created by directly linking major amino groups to the boundaries of the dots [11]. This leads to the production of GQDs with a high and focused photoluminescence efficiency.According to reports, main amine and nitrogen-carbon bonding can be quickly and simply introduced to passivate CQDs [11]. Carbon dots undergo a chemical change when they absorb light, as electrons shift from the HOMO to the LUMO. Carbon quantum dots possess a high ability to absorb light in the ultraviolet–visible because of strong tendency of the sp2 carbon–carbon double bond to occur the π-π* transition in the inner core. Furthermore, CQDs also demonstrate remarkable effectiveness in the collection and transportation of electrons. The close distance between charge carriers, often smaller than the carrier diffusion length, enhances the optical properties of CQDs. This improvement is mostly attributed to the dimensions of CQDs. The strong features of the edges of CQDs make them very useful for photocatalysis, since the oxidation reaction happens more often there than at the base planes [12,13].

Choosing red-emissive carbon quantum dots (R-CQDs) over other types like blue (B-CQDs), green (G-CQDs), or yellow (Y-CQDs) for photocatalysis is crucial for several reasons: a) Optimal Light Absorption: R-CQDs exhibit strong absorption in the visible light range, particularly around 512 nm, making them highly effective for photocatalysis under visible light conditions. This gives them an advantage over B-CQDs or G-CQDs, which are more active in the visible range, limiting their practical applications under natural sunlight; b) Efficient Electron-Hole Separation: R-CQDs tend to have a narrower band gap compared to B-CQDs or G-CQDs, allowing them to facilitate better charge separation and reduce recombination rates. This enhances the efficiency of photocatalytic reactions, making them more effective at degrading pollutants like methylene blue (MB); c) Stability and Photoluminescence: R-CQDs show excellent photostability and tunable photoluminescence properties, which are important in sustaining long-term photocatalytic performance. Their red emission also indicates the presence of certain oxygenated functional groups, which can enhance catalytic activity by participating in redox reactions more effectively than other CQDs [6,14].
Thus, the unique optical properties of R-CQDs make them more suitable for applications in visible-light-driven photocatalysis than other types like B-, G-, or Y-CQDs.

In this research, we develop a straightforward, solvothermal approach for synthesizing R-CQDs with good photostability utilizing 1,3-naphthalenediol. The as-synthesized R-CQDs are characterized using a UV–Vis, PL, Raman, XPS, TEM, FTIR techniques. R-CQDs show high photocatalytic performance for the photodegradation efficiency of 96 % in 180 min and uniform performance as an improved photocatalyst in a variety of pH media. Its photocatalytic degradation rate constant was calculated as 0.0167 min−1. Our results reveal that the R-CQDs have a potential application as a co-catalyst on photocatalysis.

1,3-naphthalenediol was purchased from Aladdin. KJO4, methyl alcohol, ethyl alcohol, dichloromethane and MB were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All experiments used deionized water and all chemicals were analytically pure.

Red emissive carbon quantum dots (R-CQDs) synthesis was carried out from our previously reported method with some modifications using the one-step solvothermal technique[15]. A transparent solution was formed by dissolving 20 mg of 1,3-naphthalenediol and 70 mg of KJO4 in 40 mL of ethyl alcohol. The transparent solution was moved autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 90 min and then cooled to room temperature. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min, and the supernatant was collected after filtering through a 0.22 µm membrane to eliminate impurities and bigger particles. The reddish solution was achieved by silica gel column chromatography, employing methanol and dichloromethane as solvents. The powder of R-CQDs was obtained that subsequently dried under vacuum freezer-drying conditions at a temperature of −50 °C for 5 days. The R-CQDs solution was prepared by dissolving a concentration of 2 mg/mL of powder in ethyl alcohol. This solution exhibits bright red fluorescence when exposed to UV light in daytime.

The photocatalytic degradation of MB dye was investigated under visible light (PLS-SXE-300C Xenon lamp, visible-light intensity of 158 mW/cm2, λex > 420 nm). Initially, 50 mL of MB dye with 20 mg/L concentration was taken in a beaker. Then 10 mL of R-CQDs having a dose of 20 mg/L was added to the prepared MB dye solution and stir it for 60 min under the dark condition to achieve the adsorption and desorption equilibrium between R-CQDs and MB dye. Then the solution was kept under a visible light source (PLS-SXE-300C Xenon lamp with cut-of-filter λex > 420 nm) to initiate the photocatalytic procces. Every 20 min, we took 1 mL of the solution and filtered it by 0.22 μm filter paper and analysed by UV–visible spectrometer (SHIMADZU UV–Vis spectrometer) to record the absorbance as a measure of the concentration of MB dye. The experiment was repeated for R-CQDs as well.

The percentage of degradation (efficiency) was determined using the following equation
(1)
degradation
efficiency
%
=
C 0
-
C t
C 0
× 100
C0 represents the initial concentration of MB dye prior to exposure to light, while Ct represents the concentration at a specific time t following the light exposure.

Making the working electrode involved putting a 40 μL R-CQDs slurry on 1 cm2 of clean FTO glasses. The slurry consisted of 5 mg of R-CQDs and 40 μL of a 5 wt% Nafion solution dissolved in 1 mL of a mixed solvent containing 3 parts water and 1 part isopropanol. We used an electrochemical workstation (CHI-660D) in a standard three-electrode system to conduct the electrochemical measurements. Mott-Schottky plot measured by using the platinum plate as the counter electrode and the reference Ag/AgCl electrode in a 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution.

A Shimadzu UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used for UV–Vis absorption analysis. For fluorescence analysis, use a Perkin Elmer LS-55 spectrophotometer. Raman micro-spectroscopy for Raman spectra, Bruker TENSOR27 FTIR for FTIR spectroscopy, JEOL 2100 (HRTEM) operational at 200 kV for morphological analysis. The Bruker-D8 technique was used for X-ray diffraction analysis, the CHI-660D was used for Mott-Schottky measurements, and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer was used for XPS spectra.

Fig. 1 a schemes the synthesis process of R-CQDs. Using potassium periodate and 1,3-naphthalenediol as a carbon source, a solvothermal treatment approach is used to create red emissive CQDs. The initial compounds were dissolved in ethanol, transferred into a Teflon reactor and then solvothermal treated for 90 min at 180 °C to give a brown solution, indicating the formation of CQDs. Methyl alcohol and dichloromethane combined as the eluent were used in silica gel column chromatography to purify the obtained red solution. After several days of vacuum freezer-drying at −50 °C, the resultant powder was obtained. The red color of the CQDs solution was achieved through the dissolution of 2 mg/mL of R-CQD powder in ethyl alcohol. When the R-CQDs were synthesised as is, they could be dissolved in ethanol to create clear red solutions that bright red when exposed to UV light. The R-CQDs' morphology and size were examined using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, as depicted in Fig. 1b, as per the HRTEM image. The R-CQDs show an average size of around 5 nm. The HRTEM image shown in Fig. 1b demonstrates that the R-CQDs can be evenly distributed in alcohol, exhibiting a red color. This distribution conforms to the specified size requirements for carbon quantum dots. The distribution satisfies the size requirements for carbon quantum dots. The structural and morphological characteristics of the R-CQDs was principally determined using XRD patterns investigations, as depicted in Fig. 1c. XRDpatterns of R-CQDs display a broad peak at a 2θ value of approximately 23.5°, which corresponds to the (002) lattice plane of graphite [16,17].

Red Carbon Quantum Dots have unique characteristics compared to other types of CQDs (like B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs) when analyzed using FTIR, Raman, and XPS techniques. The surface of R-CQDs was analyzed using FTIR analysis to provide a more detailed understanding of the functional groups present. Fig. 2 a shows the FTIR spectrum of R-CQDs in 4000–500 cm−1. The FTIR spectrum exhibits three distinct absorption peaks at 3430 cm−1, 1630 cm−1, and 1460 cm−1. These peaks correspond to the stretching vibrations of hydrogen-bonded water molecules and hydroxyl groups, as well as the bending vibrations of the O-H group and C-O group [18]. The specific peaks of R-CQDs appear at 2974, 2932, and 1580 cm−1. They are linked to the variation of the skeletal structure of sp2 and sp3 C–H and C-O bonds in the curve [13]. These functional groups contribute to the high stability and dispersibility of R-CQDs in water, as well as their ability to engage in photocatalytic reactions by trapping electrons and holes. In comparison, B-CQDs and G-CQDs may show fewer oxygenated groups and more aliphatic (C–H) or aromatic carbon (C=C) peaks. These differences can result in reduced photocatalytic activity under visible light, as they are less effective in participating in redox reactions. Raman scattering is more responsive to the carbon state or structure of CQDs in comparison to XRD [19]. Raman spectra of R-CQDs illustrated in Fig. 2b. The Raman spectrum of R-CQDs exhibits the typical D band at 1347 cm−1 and G band at 1588 cm−1. The G band, which is attributed to the sp2 hybridization of carbon, is the dominant band in the spectrum. On the other hand, the D band is linked to the presence of sp3 defect hybridization of carbon. Remarkably, the ratio of the strength of the G band peak to the D band is 1.6, above the values previously observed for graphene quantum dots [20,21]. These signify the successful construction of CQDs with a sp2 core and a sp3 hybridized matrix using the solvothermal method. The ratio of the intensity of the D to G band is often higher in R-CQDs compared to other types of CQDs, which can enhance their photocatalytic properties by providing more active sites for electron transfer. Other CQDs: B-CQDs and G-CQDs may have lower band ratios, indicating a higher graphitic content and fewer functional groups [22]. While this could lead to better electronic conductivity, it may limit their photocatalytic activity since fewer defect sites are available for redox reactions.

XPS spectroscopy is employed to analyse the surface chemistry of R-CQDs. XPS spectrum result provides information about the chemical state, and electronic state of atoms within a material. The XPS results show that the various functional groups presented in R-CQDs have an elemental composition of 72.63 % and 27.37 %, corresponding to C and O respectively (Fig. 2c). C1S spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows two peaks with binding energies at 284.6 and 285.7 eV, which corresponds to C-C/C=C (graphite) and C=O group respectively in R-CQDs [6,16,23]. In the O1s spectrum of R-CQDs, Fig. 2f exhibitions two different peaks in binding energy at 531.1 and 532.5 eV, which correspond to the C–O and O–H bonds present in R-CQDs [24,25]. These results are consistence with the FTIR results. These oxygen-containing groups are critical for enhancing the photocatalytic ability by aiding in the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. The elemental composition in R-CQDs often shows a higher oxygen content compared to other type CQDs, which increases their effectiveness in photocatalytic applications. Other CQDs: typically exhibit lower oxygen content in their XPS spectra. The C1s peak in these CQDs may primarily show C=C and C–C bonds, which indicates fewer active sites for photocatalysis due to lower surface functionalization. In summary, R-CQDs are more heavily functionalized with oxygenated groups (as shown in FTIR and XPS), and exhibit better light absorption properties. These characteristics make R-CQDs more effective in photocatalytic applications compared to B-CQDs, G-CQDs, and Y-CQDs.

CQDs are recognized for their remarkable and unique attributes, such as a wide spectrum of light emission that ranges from UV to NIR, adjustable fluorescence, and efficient multiphoton down-conversion and up-conversion. The optical properties of the R-CQDs were examined by UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) experiments. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of R-CQDs (Fig. 3 a) have an exceptionally wide absorption peak, covering almost the full visible area, at about 512 nm. This indicates the presence of strong conjugation and excellent quality in R-CQDs [14,26]. This property makes them highly effective for visible-light photocatalysis.

B-CQDs and G-CQDs typically show absorption peaks in the 300–400 nm and 400–500 nm ranges, respectively. B-CQDs absorb strongly in the UV region, which limits their use in visible-light applications. G-CQDs absorb some visible light, but their range is not ideal for full visible-light-driven applications [27]. CQDs typically exhibit n → π* and π → π* transitions in most situations due to their easy availability transition energies [28]. The π-states of carbon quantum dots are associated with the aromatic sp2-hybridized carbons located in their core. The difference between π-states and aromatic rings reduces progressively as the number of aromatic rings increases, similar to the trend observed for π-conjugation in organic compounds [29]. The PL spectrum of R-CQDs, shown in Fig. 3a inset, demonstrates an excitation-dependent feature. The optimal excitation occurs at 510 nm, while the emission appears strongest at 610 nm. This indicates that CQDs exhibit a highly advantageous characteristic of adjustable photoluminescence, enabling its use in various fields such as photocatalytic materials. PL emission for other types of CQDs occurs in the blue region (around 450 nm) or the green region (around 500–550 nm). They have limited excitation tunability, as their emission is primarily fixed at shorter wavelengths.

Photoluminescencetesting was conducted to examine the optical characteristics ofR-CQDs using various excitation wavelengths, as illustrated in Fig. 3b. The R-CQDs display photoluminescence that difference excitationdepending on the excitation. The highest emission intensity of R-CQDs is at 610 nm, which is the strongest emission wavelength. The findings indicate that the photoluminescent behavior of carbon dots is closely linked to the quantity of oxygen-containing functional groups. The extent of surface oxidation, which is influenced by the synthesis method, determines the quantity of oxygenated groups formed during the solvothermal preparation process, which leads to the partial disintegration of the C=C conjugated structure. At the same time, the band gap of R-CQDs is determined by calculating the Kubelka − Munk function. The band gap energy of the R-CQDs is estimated to be 2.11 eV, as illustrated in Fig. 3c. Remarkably, the absorption transitions of CQDs exhibit a red shift, suggesting that the electronic bandgaps of the CQDs synthesized in this study are less than those of previous fluorescence CQDs derived from precursors used in the field of photocatalysis.

In order to provide a more detailed explanation of the R-CQDs, we conducted electrochemical studies for the Mott-Schottky plot. The working electrode was fabricated by depositing a solution of R-CQDs slurry onto the pre-cleaned FTO glasses with a surface area of 1 cm2. The slurry consisted of R-CQDs and 5 wt% Nafion solution. The Mott-Schottky plot was measured with platinum plate as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution. Fig. 3d demonstrates a significant decrease in the positive slope value of R-CQDs as it approaches negative volume. The Mott-Schottky graphs at a frequency of 500 demonstrate that R-CQDs have a positive slope value of −0.7 eV compared to the saturated Ag/AgCl reference electrode at pH=7. The decrease in slope value for R-CQDs suggests an enhancement in donor density for photocatalysis, which can be attributed to the enhanced separation of charges after decorating certain semiconductors with R-CQDs. Increased donor density, potentially resulting from red-emission CQDs, enhances the photocatalytic efficiency by enhancing electrical conductivity and charge carrier mobility. This work demonstrates that the band gap of semiconductors may be adjusted by using R-CQDs, making them suitable for photocatalytic applications. Further research will be conducted to elucidate this mechanism in the future.

We tested the photocatalytic degradation of MB using a solution containing 50 mL of MB at a concentration of 20 mg/L and 10 mL of R-CQDs at the same concentration. The experiment aimed to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the as-fabricated R-CQDs under visible light, specifically with a cut-off filter that only allowed wavelengths greater than 420 nm. The MB dye solution was stirred for 60 min in the dark to achieve adsorption and desorption equilibrium between R-CQDs and MB dye. After initiating the photocatalytic process, 1 mL of the solution was extracted at 20-minute intervals to measure the concentration of MB dye by recording its absorbance. The MB dye exhibits a peak of maximum absorption at a wavelength of 664 nm. Fig. 4 a displays the UV-spectrum of MB dye with R-CQDs. The absorption peak at 664 nm exhibits a gradual decrease as the irradiation time increases. Fig. 4b illustrates the normalized changes in the concentration of MB in an aqueous solution under visible light withoutphotocatalysis, R-CQDs in the dark, and CQDs under visible light irradiation (C/C0). The concentration of MB in the CQDs gradually decreased over a period of 180 min when exposed to visible light, showing nearly complete degradation. The lack of reaction of the R-CQDs towards MB in the absence of light indicates that degradation, rather than adsorption, was the underlying process. Regarding RCQDs, exposing them to 180 min of visible light leads to a degradation efficiency of 96 %. On the other hand, with MB, the process of decolorization reaches a point of saturation after this duration. The degradation rate is determined by fitting it to a Langmuir-Hinshelwood degradation rate model defined by the following equation, where K represents the rate constant for the pseudo-first-order reaction (min−1).
(1)
L n
C 0
/ C
= K t

Fig. 4d illustrates a linear regression analysis of the given equation, where the gradient of the ln (C0/C) vs. time graph represents the rate constant. According to the data presented in Fig. 4d, the photocatalytic degradation of MB by R-CQDs followed the pseudo-first-order kinetic model. The correlation coefficient for this model was 0.9772 and the rate constant was 0.0167/min. Based on an evaluation of photocatalytic performance and degradation rate, it can be concluded that R-CQDs exhibited a higher level of photocatalytic activity for the breakdown of MB dye. The degradation process includes the adsorption of MB onto the surface of CQDs. Light energy stimulates the electrons in the valence band of CQDs, causing them to move to the conduction band and creating electron-hole pairs.

We examined the impact of the pH of the MB pollutant solution on the photocatalytic activity. To modify the pH of the pollutant solution, we employ diluted HCl or NaOH to achieve pH values of 3, 7, and 10. Fig. 4e illustrates the degrading efficiency of R-CQDs. We see that the degradation efficiency stays consistent across various pH levels. The increase in hydroxyl surface groups is responsible for the negative charge of RCQDs. The RCQDs function as a surface trap, therefore enhancing the effectiveness of separating photo-generated charge carriers. R-CQDs exhibit similar results across all pH levels, with their degrading efficiency reaching an equilibrium level of 94.6 % within a 180-min period. Researchers have discovered that CQDs are highly efficient photocatalysts for breaking down MB dye in different pH conditions. This is caused by the extensive surface areas of carbon quantum dots and the abundance of carbon-based species including negatively charged O atoms, OH, and COOH in CQDs. Although CQDs exhibited limited efficacy in dark reaction adsorption, they demonstrated exceptional proficiency in dye removal. The presence of a small number of dyes adsorbed on the surface can function as a dye sensitizer, causing the degradation of dyes on the surface upon exposure to light. Fig. 4f illustrates a graphical representation of the degradation of MB utilizing R-CQDs when exposed to visible light. When the R-CQDs are irradiated with visible light, electrons and holes are photogenerated. After that, the electrons decrease O2 to make oxygen radicals (O2 •), while the holes either directly oxidize MB or oxidize H2O to produce hydroxyl radicals (OH•). These radical species activate oxidation–reduction processes that result in the breakdown of MB caused by R-CQDs.

A one-step solvothermal method for synthesizing red-emissive R-CQDs using 1,3-naphthalenediol as a carbon source has been demonstrated. The resulting R-CQDs have a size of 5 nm, a wide absorption peak around 512 nm, the strongest emission wavelength around 610 nm and high photocatalytic performance with a 96 % efficiency in 180 min. Its photocatalytic degradation rate constant was calculated as 0.0167 min − 1. The pH of the MB dye affects their effectiveness, but R-CQDs function almost the same in all pH conditions. They exhibit superior photoluminescence, up-conversion, narrower band gap, electron acceptance, and transmission capabilities. R-CQDs could facilitate basic research and enhance the practical applications in photocatalysis.

Shokir Khojiev: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Gulmira Khojieva: Methodology, Investigation. Dan Chen: Formal analysis. Jun Chen: Methodology, Investigation. Hemdan S.H. Mohamed: Investigation. Jing Liu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Yu Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Shokir Khojiev reports financial support was provided by Wuhan University of Technology. Shokir Khojiev reports a relationship with Center for Advanced Technology, Uzbekistan that includes: board membership. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Program of Modernizing Uzbekistan National Innovation System (REP-03032022/186) founded by World Bank, Science Technology & Innovation Funding Authority (STDF, Egypt) under grant (47280), National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275142, 22293022, U22B6011), Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities-Plan 111 from the Ministry of Science and Technology and the Ministry of Education of China (B20002).
